name: Kon
description: Kon was a creator god in Incan mythology, preceding Inti, the sun god, and Viracocha, the great creator. Often depicted as a coastal deity, Kon was responsible for shaping the landscape of the Peruvian coast, though his methods were somewhat chaotic. He is said to have created the first humans, but he cursed them by removing their ability to walk uphill easily, forcing them to struggle and work hard for their sustenance. Some accounts portray him as a trickster god, while others emphasize his role as a bringer of drought and hardship. Ultimately, Kon was overthrown by Pachacamac, another powerful deity who reshaped the world and replaced Kon's people with a new generation. His presence is primarily felt in the arid coastal regions of Peru, where his legacy is intertwined with the challenges of survival in a harsh environment. He embodies the unpredictable nature of the natural world and the constant struggle for existence. Kon's influence is also reflected in the legends surrounding the creation of the islands off the coast, said to be the remnants of his battles or creations. While not as widely revered as Inti or Viracocha, Kon remains an important figure in understanding the complex tapestry of Incan beliefs and the cyclical nature of creation and destruction. He represents a primordial force, a raw and untamed aspect of the divine that was eventually superseded by more benevolent deities.
subtitle: Coastal creator god, bringer of hardship.

keyMyths.title: Kon's Creation of the Coast
keyMyths.description: Kon shaped the Peruvian coast, creating its deserts and valleys. His actions, though creative, also brought hardship to the land.

keyMyths.title: The First Humans and Kon's Curse
keyMyths.description: Kon created the first humans but cursed them to struggle by removing their ability to walk easily uphill. This forced them to work for survival.

keyMyths.title: Kon's Defeat by Pachacamac
keyMyths.description: Pachacamac, a later deity, overthrew Kon and reshaped the world, replacing Kon's people with a new generation. This marked a shift in divine power.

sources.author: Maria Rostworowski de Diez Canseco
sources.description: Provides context and analysis of Incan mythology and deities.
